title: Vegan scones
subtitle: Plant-based cream scones with a good rise
excerpt: Vegan scones use plant-based butter and a plant milk curdled with lemon juice in place of buttermilk. The result is a scone with the same short, crumbly crumb and high rise as a traditional version. Oat milk and coconut cream give the richest result. No eggs.
sourceNotes: Scone-making without dairy relies on the same chemistry as conventional scones: fat shortens the gluten, carbon dioxide from raising agents lifts the dough, and acid from the soured plant milk activates the bicarbonate of soda. Plant-based butters and milks have been refined sufficiently since the 2000s that the standard scone method translates cleanly, and vegan scone recipes now appear routinely in mainstream British baking publications.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the baked scones. Reheat from frozen at 160 degrees C fan for 10 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten the day they are made.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 12 to 15 minutes until risen and pale gold on top and the base sounds hollow

content.text: Sour the plant milk before you start: stir a tablespoon of lemon juice into 160 ml oat milk and leave for 5 minutes until slightly thickened and curdled. This mimics the acidic tang of buttermilk and activates the bicarbonate of soda. Oat milk gives a neutral flavour; coconut cream enriches the crumb. Cold plant butter rubbed into the flour behaves like dairy butter and produces the same flaky, short result.

Mix 1 tablespoon of lemon juice into 160 ml oat milk and leave for 5 minutes. Heat the oven to 210 degrees C fan. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Sift 250 g self-raising flour, 1 tsp baking powder, and a quarter teaspoon of salt into a large bowl. Rub in 55 g cold diced plant butter until the mixture resembles coarse crumbs. Stir in 50 g caster sugar. Pour in the soured oat milk and stir with a round-bladed knife until a soft dough forms. Do not overwork.

Turn the dough onto a lightly floured surface and pat to about 2.5 cm thick. Cut out rounds with a 6 cm cutter, pressing straight down without twisting. Place on the prepared tray close together. Brush the tops with a little extra oat milk. Bake for 12 to 15 minutes until risen and pale gold on top. The base should sound hollow when tapped. Rest for 5 minutes.

items.solution: The dough was overworked, or the plant butter was too soft. Keep the butter cold and handle the dough as little as possible. Press the cutter straight down without twisting to avoid sealing the cut edges.

items.problem: Scones have a slightly soapy aftertaste
items.solution: Some plant butters have a strong flavour that can come through. Switch brands, or use coconut cream (the solid part from a chilled tin) in place of plant butter.
